hero_summary: Shaar is a maqam of the Sikah family whose tonic is Sikah (E half-flat). In the nearest practical formulation, it consists of a lower Sikah jins and an upper Nahawand jins on Nawa (G).
short_identity: A Sikah-family maqam that resolves on Sikah (E half-flat) and is distinguished by ascending toward Nahawand on Nawa while keeping its first jins truly Sikah rather than Mustaar.

quick_facts.character: A Sikah counterpart to Mustaar, with a clear Nahawand upper region
quick_facts.common_start: It begins by stabilizing the Sikah tonic, then gradually rises until the Nahawand area on Nawa (G) becomes clear

sayr.summary: Shaar is a maqam of the Sikah family, and its tonic is Sikah (E half-flat). It is mentioned much less often in common references than Huzam or Mustaar, yet it appears in Arabic teaching materials as a maqam in its own right among the Sikah branches. The nearest practical formulation suggests that it resembles Mustaar in the upper jins, but differs from it in the lower one: Mustaar begins with Jins Mustaar, whereas Shaar begins with Jins Sikah.
resting_tones: Sikah (E half-flat): the tonic and main center of attraction
Nawa (G): an important upper center because it carries the Nahawand jins that gives the maqam its upper identity
Jaharkah (F) and Husayni (A) may appear as passing or temporary resting tones, but the real weight remains between the tonic Sikah and the upper Nawa
motion_notes: If the listener does not clearly hear Sikah (E half-flat) as the tonic, the maqam’s identity may blur into Mustaar or into other Sikah-family branches
If Nahawand on Nawa (G) does not become clear above, the character of Shaar may remain incomplete
The maqam is not reducible to a mere scale, but is defined by the relationship between a lower Sikah jins and an upper Nahawand jins
The decisive difference from Mustaar remains in the first jins: if the opening is Mustaar, the maqam is Mustaar; if the opening is Sikah, the maqam is Shaar
common_path: Stabilize the tonic on Sikah (E half-flat)
Ascend gradually until the Nahawand region on Nawa (G) becomes audible
Bring out Nawa-Nahawand clearly so the maqam’s distinct profile appears
Return to the tonic without losing the Sikah identity of the maqam

common_modulations: The maqam closest to Shaar is Mustaar: both share Nahawand on Nawa (G) above, but Mustaar begins with Jins Mustaar, whereas Shaar begins with Jins Sikah
Sikah is the broader original family, whereas Shaar is a more specific branch characterized by directing the upper region toward Nahawand on Nawa
Huzam also belongs to the Sikah family, but is usually defined through Hijaz in the upper region rather than Nahawand on Nawa as in Shaar

The scholarly caution here concerns not the existence of the maqam or its Sikah affiliation, but the relative weakness of strong academic documentation under this exact name when compared with more famous maqamat
